pattern: Only the first and last character of each word matter, so a single pass over the requested window checking both ends against the vowel set answers the whole question.
hints: The interior of each word is irrelevant — only whether its very first and very last letters are vowels decides if it counts.
Walk just the words at positions left through right, inclusive, rather than the whole array.
For each of those words, check membership of its first and last character in the fixed set {a, e, i, o, u} and tally how many satisfy both.

constraints.note: each word length 1..10, lowercase English letters only

edge_inputs.description: single-letter words, each one is its own vowel or consonant
left equals right, a window of exactly one word
a word starting with a vowel but ending in a consonant does not count
a word ending with a vowel but starting with a consonant does not count
every word in the window is a vowel string
none of the words in the window are vowel strings

stress.description: random lowercase words and a random inclusive window
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    import string
    n = min(1000, max(1, size))
    words = []
    for _ in range(n):
        length = rng.randint(1, 10)
        words.append(''.join(rng.choice(string.ascii_lowercase) for _ in range(length)))
    left = rng.randint(0, n - 1)
    right = rng.randint(left, n - 1)
    return [words, left, right]
